name: Integers — Numbers Below Zero
whatIsIt: Numbers don't stop at zero. INTEGERS include the counting numbers (1, 2, 3…), zero, AND their opposites below zero (−1, −2, −3…). On a two-way number line, positives go RIGHT of zero and negatives go LEFT. A minus sign means 'the opposite' — below zero, owed, or below a starting point.
whyNeeded: Real life goes below zero all the time: temperature on a cold morning, money you owe, floors below the ground, a submarine under the sea. Integers let us record and calculate with 'less than nothing', and they open the door to algebra and coordinates.
realLifeUses: Temperature of −3 °C on a winter morning in Shimla
A lift going to floor −2 (two floors below ground)
Owing ₹50 shown as −50 in an account
A submarine 20 m below sea level = −20 m

vocabulary.meaning: A whole number that can be positive, negative, or zero.
A number less than zero, written with a minus sign (−).
The same distance from zero on the other side (+5 and −5).

story.title: The Cold Morning Report
story.text: In Leh the thermometer reads −4 °C at dawn. By noon it climbs 9 degrees. 'What does it read now?' asks Grandpa. Tenzin pictures a number line: start at −4, jump 9 steps to the right. He lands on +5. 'It's 5 °C now!' Grandpa smiles: 'Below zero and back — that's integers at work.'
story.extractedProblem: The temperature starts at −4 °C and rises 9 degrees. What is the new temperature?
story.answerInStory: 5 °C — start at −4 and move 9 steps right to +5.

visual.caption: A two-way number line: negatives left of 0, positives right. Adding moves right, subtracting moves left.

teachingGallery.title: Read and move on the number line

examples.caption: −3 and +3 are opposites, equally far from 0.

standardMethod.summary: Place integers on a two-way number line; to add move right, to subtract move left; the further left, the smaller.
steps: Draw zero in the middle: negatives to the left, positives to the right.
To ADD a positive, move RIGHT; to subtract (or add a negative), move LEFT.
To COMPARE, the number further to the right is bigger (so −2 > −5).
Opposites (+n and −n) are the same distance from zero.

workedExamples.problem: A diver is at −15 m and rises 6 m. What is her new depth?
steps: Start at −15 on the number line.
On the number line, −7 is further LEFT than −2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking −7 is bigger than −2 because 7 is bigger than 2.
commonMistakes.fix: For negatives it flips: the further LEFT of zero, the SMALLER. So −7 < −2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting the minus sign, so −9 gets written or read as 9.
commonMistakes.fix: The minus sign changes everything — it means below zero. Always keep it.

easy.q: Which number is to the LEFT of zero on the number line?
Start at 0 and go 4 steps left. Where do you land? (write like -4)

medium.q: Temperature −5 °C rises by 8 degrees. What is it now? (write like 3)

challenge.q: A lift at floor −2 goes up 5 floors. Which floor now? (write like 3)
Ravi says −8 is greater than −3 'because 8 > 3'. What's the fix?

options.label: −3 is greater — it's closer to zero (further right)

challenge.answer: −3 is greater — it's closer to zero (further right)

questions.q: Go 3 steps left of zero. Where? (write like -3)

hintLadder: Same distance, other side of zero.

teachBackPrompt: Draw a number line from −5 to +5 and mark zero. Show a grown-up a real 'below zero' example (temperature, a basement floor, or owing money) and explain how adding moves right and subtracting moves left across zero.

revisionCard.summary: Integers = positives, zero, and negatives. Two-way number line: negatives LEFT, positives RIGHT of zero. Add → move right, subtract → move left. Further LEFT = SMALLER, so −7 < −2. Opposites (+n, −n) are equal distances from zero.

rememberIt.hook: Number line: left = smaller, right = bigger. Negatives sit below zero
rememberIt.unpack: -5 is LESS than -2 (further left). Adding moves right, subtracting moves left. Two minuses touching make a plus: -(-3) = +3.

studentQuestions.a: Smaller. On the number line -5 is further left. Think temperature: -5 degrees is colder than -2.

studentQuestions.q: Why does minus a minus become plus?
studentQuestions.a: Taking away a loss makes you better off. Removing a -3 (a debt) adds +3.
